title: What a God Cost
subtitle: A Temple Budget from Oil, Linen, Grain, Taxes, and Priests in Egyptian Documents
summary: What did it actually cost to keep a temple running in Egypt? A set of papyri from the second century BCE to the third century CE lets us answer with unusual precision: priests had to be supervised and taxed, temple enclosures carried charges, oil presses generated revenue, sacred staff consumed daily grain, gods needed fine linen robes, kyphi, incense, myrrh, and balsam, lamplighters had to guarantee the daily oil supply, and even priestly admission had a price. This is the economics of cult stripped down to line items.

sections.title: The Account
sections.body_html:
The document now known as P.Louvre 1.4 reads like the middle pages of a ledger after the explanatory cover sheet has gone missing.1 It records taxes paid to the account of the administration and then starts naming categories.

Oversight of priests: 5,500 drachmas.1

Two temple enclosures at Nilopolis, one of Isis Nepherses, one of Isis Nephremmis: 2,100 drachmas, plus surcharge.1

A fee to the village secretary: 95 drachmas, plus surcharge.1

Oil presses of Socnopaeus at Socnopaiou Nesos: 142 drachmas 2 1/2 obols, plus surcharge.1

For the offices of prophet, lesones, and image-bearer of the gods Socnopaeus and Enoupis at Nilopolis: 64 drachmas, plus surcharge.1

For the prophet of the very great god Suchus: 344 drachmas.1

Then, under taxes subject to the epistrategos, the account shifts into a slightly different register: fullers, weighing-toll, embalmers, vegetable sellers, beer tax, fishing boats.1 And then, without any embarrassment at all, it moves into direct cult provisioning:

3 fine linen robes for the gods: 300 drachmas total.1

Kyphi and other expenses: 500 drachmas.1

Kyphi for the god Harpocrates: 96 drachmas.1

Sacrifice and incense on the birthday of the deified Augusti: 40 drachmas.1

Myrrh and balsam for the gods on 3 days: 60 drachmas.1

Then comes the food line. The account specifies baking for the officiating priests of the five tribes of the great god Socnopaeus, 1 artaba of wheat daily, which totals 365 artabas per year before festival extras are even added.1 Feast days add more baking in blocks of seven, eight, nine, or nineteen days, often at 4 artabas daily, for celebrations including the birthday of Socnopaeus, the marriage of Isis Nepherses, and other recurring cult dates.1

This is a real operating budget. Not a metaphorical one. A real one.

sections.title: What the Biggest Costs Actually Were
sections.body_html:
If we sort the line items by economic function rather than by the order in which the papyrus lists them, six major cost centers emerge.

  1. Administrative oversight and official fees
  2. Sacred personnel
  3. Fuel and light
  4. Textiles and image care
  5. Aromatics and ritual consumables
  6. Food and festival provisioning

The first surprise is that the account does not begin with sacrifice or incense. It begins with management. Oversight of priests is one of the biggest numbers on the page.1 That tells us something important about cult under the Ptolemies and Romans. Temples were expensive not only because they worshipped but because they were supervised. The state, local administration, and the scribal machinery around sacred institutions all extracted money from the fact that priests existed and had to be monitored.14

The second surprise is that personnel appear both directly and indirectly. Offices such as prophet and image-bearer have identifiable fiscal weight.1 But so do the priests as eaters. An institution that had to bake 365 artabas of wheat per year for officiants was not paying only in coin. It was maintaining a permanent grain burden.1

And the third surprise is that some of the most mundane items – oil, robes, secretary’s fees – are exactly what turn cult from isolated devotion into infrastructure.

sections.title: Oil: The Daily Drain
sections.body_html:
Temples burned money because they burned oil.

That is not rhetorical. One late first-century BCE oath from Oxyrhynchus shows how formally this could be organized. Four men – two lamplighters for the temple of Sarapis and the Isis-shrine, and two lamplighters for the temple of Thoeris – swear that they will superintend the lamps and supply the proper oil for the daily lamps burning in the temples for the whole year.2 They bind not only themselves but all their property as security for performance.2

This is a service contract with collateral. The lamps must burn daily. The oil must be correct in quantity and kind. The workers are mutually liable. If they fail, they lose more than face.2

Now put that beside P.Louvre 1.4, where the oil presses of Socnopaeus at Socnopaiou Nesos appear as a revenue-bearing and taxable asset.1 The picture becomes clearer. Temples did not merely consume oil. They were also entangled in its production and processing. A sanctuary could own or control oil presses, derive income from them, and still have to guarantee the constant flow of lamp oil into ritual use.12

This is one of the clearest examples of sacred institutions behaving like vertically integrated operations. They used the commodity. They taxed or controlled the equipment that produced it. They appointed personnel whose work depended on it. And because oil was daily rather than occasional, the pressure was constant.12

A much later scrap asking a priest to supply one sextarius of olive oil to a colleague shows the same logic in miniature.5 Oil was always one delivery away from shortage.

sections.title: Clothing the Gods
sections.body_html:
The gods were not naked, and that had a price.

P.Louvre 1.4 includes 3 fine linen robes for the gods costing 300 drachmas.1 This is not decoration tucked away in a literary flourish. It is a line item, and probably not a one-off one. In Egyptian cult, clothing the divine image was a real act of service. Images had to be dressed, undressed, stored, repaired, and replaced. The office of stolistes existed because textiles around a cult image mattered enough to require a specialist.6

We see that office explicitly in 216 CE when Aurelios Pakysis, priest and stolistes of the temple of the first rank at Soknopaiou Nesos, identifies himself in an official declaration.6 The declaration is not a textile account, but the title matters. It tells us that vestment management was not a side chore casually absorbed by a priest. It was a recognized office in the hierarchy of temple labor.6

A more brutal glimpse comes from the second-century BCE petition of Poregebthis, priest and warden of the temple of Isis. After being attacked while sacrificing, he lists what was involved in the assault and theft: among other things, a linen garment worth 2,000 drachmas.7 Whether this was his own ceremonial garment, temple property temporarily in his possession, or another piece of sacred or semi-sacred textile wealth, the valuation is striking. Cloth in Egypt was money. In temple contexts it was also presence. If the god had to appear properly clothed, then linen was not optional surplus. It was operational necessity.7

sections.title: Kyphi, Incense, Myrrh, Balsam
sections.body_html:
Temples also had to smell like temples.

That sentence sounds frivolous until one looks at the numbers. Kyphi and other expenses: 500 drachmas.1 Kyphi for the god Harpocrates: 96 drachmas.1 Sacrifice and incense on the birthday of the deified Augusti: 40 drachmas.1 Myrrh and balsam for the gods on 3 days: 60 drachmas.1

Kyphi was not generic incense. It was a compounded aromatic with ingredients, labor, recipe, and cult specificity. To buy it was not like buying lamp oil or grain. It was more like buying a branded ritual technology. Myrrh and balsam belonged to the same expensive world of imported or carefully controlled aromatics. These were prestige consumables, burned away into air and therefore perpetually requiring replacement.18

This is where temple economics becomes visibly different from the economics of an ordinary landed estate. A farm needs grain, tools, labor, and animals. A temple needs all of those and then also needs fragrant substances that create the sensory difference between a sacred room and a merely inhabited one. Holiness had consumable overhead.

The wider documentary evidence confirms how monetized this sector could be. A mid-second-century BCE petition from a contractor for the retailing and tax on juniper resin complains that ibis-buriers are illegally bypassing the proper point of sale and damaging his revenue.9 Temple-adjacent specialists and ritual markets were perfectly capable of colliding with state-sanctioned monopoly. Aromatics were not just religious objects. They were contested commodities.9

sections.title: Grain: The Permanent Salary
sections.body_html:
If coin and aromatics made the budget vivid, grain made it heavy.

The most revealing part of P.Louvre 1.4 is the bread account. The officiating priests of the five tribes of the great god Socnopaeus receive baking equivalent to 1 artaba of wheat daily, totaling 365 artabas over the year.1 Then festivals add more. Seven days here. Eight there. Nineteen days for the birthday of Socnopaeus. Nine days for the marriage of Isis Nepherses. Eight days for another celebration in Choiak. Seven days for the installation of Socnopaeus. Often 4 artabas daily during those festival runs.1

This is temple payroll in grain.

Not the whole payroll. But enough to show how sacred labor could be fed directly from agricultural calculation. The priests are not only spiritually attached to the cult. They are metabolically attached to it. A shrine of any size needed a dependable grain base or a dependable way to buy one.1

That is why another, earlier papyrus matters so much. In 107-108 CE, an account of tax abatements records land sacred to Isis of Taposiris Aquila, along with the money and corn reckoned on it, including taxes, other charges, and land categories such as unsown, dry, worn-away, pasture, and planted land.3 The text is difficult and partial, but its administrative logic is unmistakable: sacred land was measured, classified, and taxed as land. Temple economy began in acreage.3

The grain line in the temple account and the sacred-land tax account belong together. A temple could feed priests because it had land, concessions, leases, abatements, or some combination thereof. The bread on the feast day is only the visible end of an agrarian chain.134

sections.title: Priests Were Revenue Objects Too
sections.body_html:
One of the least romantic documents in the corpus is a receipt for admission tax of priests from 104-105 CE.10 Someone named Pekysis, son of Satabous, paid 12 silver drachmae for the admission tax of priests at Theadelphia, plus surcharges.10

To enter priestly service, in other words, could itself be taxable.

This is the sort of fact that instantly rearranges how one thinks about religion in Roman Egypt. Priestly office was not merely honor, piety, lineage, or local standing. It was fiscally legible. It generated receipts. It had a price of entry.104

The larger accounting papyrus reinforces the same point from another side. Oversight of priests costs thousands of drachmas.1 A temple system pays for priestly administration, taxes the office, feeds the officiants in grain, dresses them and the gods in linen, and supervises the whole arrangement through local and supra-local officials.110

No serious temple budget can therefore be written as though the priests were just part of the scenery. Priests were both workers and taxable categories. Their existence created administrative load.

sections.title: Enclosures, Presses, Boats, and Side Revenues
sections.body_html:
P.Louvre 1.4 refuses to separate sacred space from the ordinary taxable world. It moves from enclosures of Isis to oil presses of Socnopaeus, then to fullers, weighing-toll, embalmers, vegetable sellers, and even taxes on fishing boats and beer.1

This is not disorder. It is the actual ecology of temple finance.

A sacred center could not survive on offerings alone. It needed rent-bearing or tax-bearing attachments. Enclosures brought charges. Oil presses brought charges. Markets and occupations clustered around temple settlements and were taxed in relation to them.14

One way to say this is that the temple was embedded in the local economy. A better way is to say that the temple was one of the machines by which the local economy was organized. If you wanted revenue, you did not need to choose between gods and presses, or gods and boats, or gods and market tolls. In the Egyptian evidence, these categories coexist comfortably on the same page.1

sections.title: Offerings Were Wealth, But Not Working Capital
sections.body_html:
One might imagine that the main wealth of a temple lay in dedications. Sometimes it did. A spectacular list from 213-216 CE, P.Oxy. 12.1449, inventories offerings across a network of temples in and around Oxyrhynchus: gold lamps, spoons, silver bracelets, mirrors, armlets, statues with marble busts and wooden bodies, bronze altars, robes, trinkets for children, and more.11

The list is wonderful because it shows what had accumulated in temple rooms over time. Some offerings are old enough that the priests no longer know who dedicated them. Some are decayed and useless. Clothing has rotted. Lamps are listed by weight. Figures are composite, part precious and part cheap.11

This is temple wealth in storage rather than temple cash flow.

Its economic meaning is subtle. Dedicatory wealth made a shrine visibly rich and materially dense. It also imposed maintenance burdens. A gold lamp is not grain. A decayed robe is a problem, not income. A statue with a marble bust and wooden body still has to be housed, protected, and maybe carried in procession.11

One line at the end of the papyrus is especially revealing: in certain villages there is provided daily 4 cotyle of oil, which goes to the imperial representations housed there.11 Even in an inventory of offerings, the story ends in operating expense. Stored wealth was impressive. Running cost was relentless.11

sections.title: What the State Wanted Most
sections.body_html:
The state wanted two things from temple economies: visibility and yield.

It wanted visibility because sacred wealth, sacred land, sacred staff, and sacred offices all had to be classified if they were to be taxed, supervised, or conceded. It wanted yield because temples sat on productive assets and organized labor, and states do not ignore productive assets and organized labor for long.4

That is why we get sacred-land abatements recorded by officials, priest admission taxes receipted by collectors, oil presses named in fiscal accounts, and line items for oversight of priests and village-secretary fees inside what modern readers would instinctively call a temple account.1310

A temple was never only a place where people met the divine. It was also a place where the state met value.

sections.title: What a God Cost
sections.body_html:
So what did a god cost?

At minimum:

  • daily oil for lamps2
  • grain for officiating priests every day of the year1
  • extra grain for festival days1
  • fine linen robes for the gods1
  • kyphi, incense, myrrh, balsam, and other aromatics1
  • wages, collateral, or formal obligations for sacred staff2
  • administrative fees, priest oversight, and secretary's charges1
  • taxes on priestly office and sacred property110
  • the upkeep or exploitation of productive assets such as land, enclosures, cattle, and oil presses1367

That is the strict answer.

The fuller answer is harder. A god cost whatever it took to make divine presence look continuous. Lamps that never failed. robes that did not embarrass the cult. priests who were fed. incense on the right day. oil on ordinary days. enough grain in storage to survive the festival calendar. enough administrative coherence to keep the state satisfied and the local network functioning. enough property to generate all of the above again next year.1211

An Egyptian temple was not expensive because people were irrational. It was expensive because visible holiness is one of the most logistics-heavy things human beings ever decided to build.

Corpus ID `43238`, `P.Oxy. 12.1453`, `30-29 BCE`: oath of four lamplighters at Oxyrhynchus, binding themselves and their property to the daily supply of proper oil for temple lamps in the shrines of Sarapis, Isis, and Thoeris.

Corpus ID `40623`, `P.Oxy. 12.1434`, `107-108 CE`: account of abatements and tax reckoning for land sacred to `Isis of Taposiris Aquila`, showing sacred land treated as a fiscal asset with money and grain obligations.

Marie-Pierre Chaufray, "Impact of the Roman Conquest on Temple Economies in Egypt: A Case Study of the Temple of Soknopaios in Dime," in *The Economy of Roman Religion* (Oxford, 2023), abstract and metadata at Oxford Academic.
Corpus ID `19050`, `ZPE 196.190.7`, `4th century CE`: brief note ordering the supply of one sextarius of olive oil through a priestly network.
Corpus ID `47354`, `BGU 1.321`, `7 April 216 CE`: declaration of `Aurelios Pakysis`, `priest and stolistes` of the first-rank temple of Soknopaiou Nesos.

Corpus ID `9681`, `P.Tebt. 3.1.797`, `2nd century BCE`: petition of `Poregebthis`, priest and warden of the temple of Isis, listing honey, a linen garment valued at `2,000 drachmas`, `328 bronze drachmas`, a bronze altar, and a vessel among the valuable goods involved in an assault.

On kyphi and temple aromatics in the Egyptian cult economy, see the Ptolemaic and Roman temple-economy overviews in Alan Bowman and Andrew Wilson, eds., *The Ancient Egyptian Economy* (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2016), especially chapters on the Ptolemaic period and institutional landholding.

Corpus ID `43235`, `P.Oxy. 12.1449`, `213-216 CE`: large inventory of offerings across multiple Oxyrhynchite temples, including gold lamps, silver objects, robes, statues, altars, and a note that `daily 4 cotyle of oil` was provided in some village shrines.

20 dr. 8 ch. Total 388 dr. 8 ch.
Paid to the account of the nomarch: tax assessed on the above mentioned fishing boats 685 dr. 4 ob. (1% ob.), decanicon of fishing boats 60 dr., beer tax at Soc, Nes, 980 dr.
3 fine linen robes for the gods: Hathyr 7 100 dr., Phamenoth 9 100 dr., Epiph 26 100 dr. Total 300 dr. Kyphi and other expenses 500 dr., kyphi for the god Harpocrates 96 dr., sacrifice and incense on the birthday of the deified Augusti 40 dr., myrrh and balsam for the gods on 3 days 60 dr.
Total 1901 dr. 1½ ob.
Total payments as above 1 talent 4700 dr. ⅓ ob.
Balance remaining from receipts 637 dr. 4 ob. & ch., which were paid to the imperial account as poll-tax for the supernumerary priests. 
Baking for the officiating priests of the five tribes of the great god Socnopaeus, 1 art. wheat daily. 365 art.
Also baking for the same priests at the festivals of the gods:

sources.translation:
Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              Copy of an oath. We, Thonis also called Patoiphis son of Thonis and Heraclides son of Totoës, both lamplighters of the temple of Sarapis, the most great god, and of the Isis-shrine there, and Paapis son of Thonis and Petosiris son of the aforesaid Patoiphis, both lamplighters of the temple of Thoëris, the most great goddess, at Oxyrhynchus, all four swear by Caesar, god and son of a god, to Heliodorus son of Heliodorus and Helio- dorus son of Ptolemaeus, overseers of the temples in the Oxyrhynchite and Cynopolite nomes, that we will superintend the lamps of the above mentioned temples, as aforesaid, and will supply the proper oil for the daily lamps burning in the temples signified from Thoth 1 to Mesore (intercalary day ?) 5 of the present 1st year of Caesar... in accordance with what was supplied up to the 22nd which was the 7th year; and we the aforesaid are mutually sureties and all our property is security for the performance of the duties herein written. If I observe the oath may it be well with me, but if I swear falsely, the reverse. The 1st year of Caesar... Copies of the signatures of the parties, those of Paapis and Petosiris being written by proxies.

Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              From Apollonius, lately appointed comogrammateus of the Monimou farmstead and other villages in the upper toparchy. Account of abatements of taxation for the 11th year of the Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, as follows. Abatements of which the due amounts in money and corn are reckoned here: to Gaius Julius, son of Gaius Julius Theon, formerly chief-priest and hypomnematographus, of the land in this nome sacred to Isis of Taposiris Aquila conceded in accordance with an application presented by Gaius Julius Theon the father, in accordance with the concession made to him by Gaius Tyrannius by an edict, from the account of Caesar, on condition that he should pay at the city for taxes and other charges 2 talents ... drachmae, at ... this in the holding of Demetrius son of Po... .. arurae, of which .. are in the category of land that is unsown or dry or worn away, .. in the pastures in the estates of the lord Augustus, in place of which there are planted with olives (?), reckoned up to the 20th year in the account of ..., . arurae; at Psobthis taxed at 1 artaba 1 arura, making 1 artaba, taxed at .. 21/64 arur., at Idu ... 3/64 arur., total 1 21/64 arur., at Idu ... 3/64 arur.; money- taxes, for naubion at 200 dr. of copper, for exchange (?) ..., for naubion (?) 221 (?) dr., of which 8(?) dr. ...

List of offerings for the 2[.] year of Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Max. Germanicus Max. Pius Augustus, as follows. Objects in the temple of Neotera, a representation of our lord the Emperor M. sources.translation:
To Aurelios Didymos strategus of the merides of Themistos and Polemon of the Arsinoite from Aurelios Pakysis son of Tesenouphis, 
priest and stolistes of the temple of the first rank of the village of Soknopaiou Nesos of the meris of Herakleides. 
 I possess a place in the house of the wife of my son Aurelios Tesenouphis in the hamlet of Pisaïs in the meris of Themistos in which I keep in storage foodstuffs for our daily use.
 Just lately my relatives went into the place because I myself was away in Alexandria and it was discovered that the foodstuffs had been pilfered. 
 The cause of the theft, as was clear, was that the crime had been committed when the place, which is on the upper floor, had been holed through the floor. 
 When those who are living there were proved to have done this through malice aforethought, they undertook in the presence of both the archephodos of the village and others to give me seven artabas of grain against the amount of the theft. 
 But since they have agreed to the undertaking but up until now have not complied with the restitution, of necessity I am making submission of a petition which I request be placed on record, 
so that my rights be secured for me against the accused, Panouphis son of Stotoetis and Pakysis son of Kanneis. Farewell.  (2nd hand) I, Aureliou (sic) Pakysis, have submitted the petition. 
 (1st hand) Year 24 of Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus Pius Augustus, Pharmouthi 12. 
Translation with minor modifications: J. Whitehorne, BASP 40 (2003), 203-204

sources.translation:
To King Ptolemy and Queen Cleopatra ...
                         greetings. I, Peep...es, son of P...kes, Arsinoite, ... the contractor for the retailing and
                         the tax upon the ... juniper resin ..., am being wronged by (?)
                         some ibis-buriers in the nome ...
                         who, on the one hand, ... numerous burials ...
                         of those selling illegally and ...
                         of some temples (sacred things?) and distribute to others ...
                         they purchased nothing at all from the royal (?) place of sale ... until
                   now it has not happened that they are punished ...
                         and the damage to me is not inconsiderable ...
                         accuse the ibis-buriers falsely ...
                         Translation: C.A. Láda – A. Papathomas, Tyche 30 (2015) 82

sources.translation:
To [..…]esis, archiphylakitês of Berenikis Thesmophorou, and his phylakitai, from Poregebthis, priest and warden of the temple of Isis 
from the same village. On the 15th of the present month, while I was sacrificing in the temple of Isis there 
on behalf of the king and queen and their children and ancestors, totally innocent, … Perutis, son of Perutis, 
whipped (me?)… and took… and when I cried out for help, Papontos, my …, but they removed by force from the… (and) 
planted many blows on me, and wounded my shin, and beat me in my face and took off in possession of my honey and a linen garment 
worth 2000 drachmas, and a bag containing 328 bronze drachmas, and a bronze altar and a drinking vessel, 
(all of?) which I recovered from the wardens of the temple on the 15th. I am submitting a petition to you so that you may send 
the perpetrators to Meleagros, the epistatês phylakitôn, so that, once the examination of them has been held, 
they may meet with the fitting punishment and I may recover the things which I have described. For once this has happened, ….
(J. Bauschatz, Law and Enforcement, p. 72)

sources.description: Letter concerning the cows of Isis and Osiris as managed assets
sources.greek: Ζήνωνι χαίρε ιν Πατῦμις Ἁραπάκτιος ἰσιονό μος. ἐπίστη ι διότι παρακαταθήκην δέδω κα τὰς βο ῦς τῆς Ἴσεως καὶ Ὀσίρεως. καλ ῶς οὖ ν ποιήσει ς, εἰ καὶ σοὶ δοκεῖ, ἣν ἄν σοι δοκῇ βοῦν βους ἐκ τούτ ων λαβεῖν, τὰς δὲ λοιπὰς ἀποδοῦναι ὅπως ἂ ν ἔχω τὸν τὸμ βίον ἐξ αὐτῶν. ἐσκέπασες ἡμᾶς κ α ὶ ἀρχῆ ς , ἔτι δὲ καὶ νῦν οὐδεὶς ἡμᾶς μὴ σκε πάσῃ ἀλλʼ ἢ σὺ οὕνεκεν τῆς Ἴσεως. οὐδενὶ γ ὰρ ἂν ἐπίστευσα ἀλλʼ ἢ σοὶ ἵνα σκεπά σῃς ἡμᾶ ς. εὐτύχει. εὐτύχει.
sources.translation: ‘To Zenon greeting from Patumis, son of Harapaktis, steward of Isis. You know that I have given to you as a deposit the cows of Isis and Osiris. Be good enough therefore, if you too agree, to take whichever one of them you please and give me back the rest in order that I may gain my living from them. You protected us in the beginning, and now again no one but you will protect us, for the sake of Isis. For I would not have put trust in anyone but you to protect us. May you prosper.’
